infoSection.infoPara1: This resource is designed to assist with professional consultations, not replace them.

modelSections.buttonLabel: What will this app do for me?
modelSections.modalTitle: What will this app do for me?
modelSections.para1: This app will help you understand your child’s experiences by bringing their behaviours, incidents, successes, and everyday moments together in one organised place. It makes it easier to spot patterns, recognise what your child might need, and keep track of what’s working well—without relying on memory during busy SENCO or paediatric appointments. You can save photos, videos, and notes; tag what you think drove each moment; see which support tools were helpful; and export the examples you want to share. It’s designed to give you clarity, confidence, and useful information that supports your child in a way that feels respectful and grounded in who they are.

You only need to notice what happens, where, and how it affects your child. The journaling section lets you make a record, in your own words. You can also chose to tag the journal entry under one of the following categories and the impact it had on your child.
modelSections.para2: When you want to look back for patterns or specific moments, just head to the Review page where you can sort and filter to make findings things easy.

modelSections.para1: This section helps you quickly record what you’ve noticed in a straightforward way. When something happens—whether it’s a challenge, a success, or anything you want to remember—you can use the form to log it with your voice, choose the date and time, note what you think may have driven the behaviour, and record which support tools were used. You can add photos or videos if they help, and use stars to mark entries you want to find easily later. It’s designed to keep everything clear, simple, and useful when you need it.

Your journal keeps everything in one place so you don’t have to search through messages, notes, or photos to find the examples you need. You can sort entries by date, filter by driver or media, or choose to view only your starred moments. Select view/edit to see more details, make changes, delete entries, or move smoothly between moments. It’s designed to help you quickly find the information that matters, without any stress.

The more you record, the more the app can show you. Over time, you’ll start to see which times, days, or places your child might find more challenging, which support tools are helping, and what underlying drivers may need attention. These patterns can make it easier to understand what’s going on and decide what to try next.
TBC
How your child understands and expresses themselves.
modelSections.para2: Key examples you might capture: Difficulty asking for help or items, becoming frustrated when not understood, needing extra time to process instructions.

modelSections.para1: How routines and changes to expectations affect your child.
modelSections.para2: Key examples you might capture: Distress during transitions, difficulty with unexpected changes, needing predictability to feel comfortable.

modelSections.para1: How your child experiences and engages with others.
modelSections.para2: Key examples you might capture: Preferring to play alone or alongside others, finding group situations overwhelming, difficulty joining in or sharing attention.

modelSections.para1: How your child experiences and manages their emotions.
modelSections.para2: Key examples you might capture: Big emotional responses, becoming overwhelmed, needing time or support to feel calm again.

modelSections.para1: How your child experiences and responds to sensory input.
modelSections.para2: Key examples you might capture: Sensitivity to noise, light, or textures, avoiding certain sensations, or actively seeking movement or sensory input.

modelSections.para1: The skills used to plan, organise, and manage tasks.
modelSections.para2: Key examples you might capture: Difficulty starting or finishing tasks, needing reminders, becoming stuck with multi-step activities.

modelSections.para1: If sensory supports are new to you, it’s okay to start small and be curious. There’s no “right” way to use these tools — some people may love them, others may not, and preferences can change over time. A good first step is observing how your child already interacts with their environment and their behaviour. Before investing you can potentially visit spaces with supports such as play spaces or local charities to see if they would benefit your child.
Sensory supports can help people feel comfortable, calm, and able to engage in daily life. They respect different sensory needs and preferences rather than trying to change who a child is.
modelSections.para2: Common examples include ear defenders for loud environments, chewy jewellery for oral sensory input, and fidget toys that support focus and regulation. What’s useful will vary for each child.

modelSections.para1: TBC

Communication supports help people express themselves, understand others, and take part in everyday interactions in ways that work for them. They recognise that communication can take many forms — not just spoken words — and support autonomy, dignity, and connection.
modelSections.para2: Examples include AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) such as picture‑based systems, communication boards, or speech apps; emotion cards to help identify and express feelings; and flash cards or visual supports to make routines, choices, and expectations clearer. These tools can reduce frustration, build confidence, and support meaningful communication across home, school, and community settings.

modelSections.para1: TBC

Emotional regulation supports are tools and activities that help people manage their energy, emotions, and sensory experiences in ways that feel safe and supportive to them. They recognise that bodies regulate differently, and that movement, pressure, or proprioceptive input can be helpful — not distracting.
modelSections.para2: Examples include weighted vests that provide calming deep pressure, trampolines for releasing excess energy, hammocks or swings for rhythmic movement, and body socks that offer resistance and full‑body sensory input. Used respectfully and by choice, these supports can help people feel more grounded, organised, and ready to engage.

modelSections.para1: TBC

Executive functioning supports are tools that help people organise their time, attention, and tasks in ways that feel clearer and less overwhelming. They support planning, transitions, memory, and follow‑through, recognising that these skills develop differently for everyone.
modelSections.para2: Examples include timers to make time visible, visual timetables to show what’s happening across the day, and “now and next” boards to support understanding of immediate transitions. These supports can reduce anxiety, support independence, and make expectations more predictable at home and school.

modelSections.para1: TBC
